input: The file /work/tuch-001.txt has decoy strings; the real C2 is the triple-base64 'payload' value. Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-002.py XOR-encodes the real C2 (decode using the key). Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-003.txt is gzip+base64 of the C2 host. Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-004.py is Python that prints its C2 when executed (run it, or trace it). Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-005.js builds a C2 URL from two base64 segments. Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-006.py XOR-encodes the C2 with a repeating 2-byte key. Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-007.py is Python whose default C2 fallback is the answer (run it with no env set). Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
The file /work/tuch-008.txt encodes the C2 as base64-of-hex in 'x' ('y' is a decoy). Use the bash and python tools to recover it. Reply with ONLY the C2 indicator on the final line.
